aliases: rice vermicelli in tamarind prawn gravy
food_subgroup: Spiced rice vermicelli in tamarind-coconut gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Mee Siam. Nearest parent would be a spiced rice noodle dish. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite noodle dishes — rice vermicelli in spiced tamarind broth (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical plate of Mee Siam in Singapore weighs approximately 360g and consists of stir-fried rice vermicelli in a spiced, sweet-sour tamarind and coconut milk gravy, topped with prawns, fried tofu, hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, chives, and a lime wedge on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Siam — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05060085
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Foodo under food code F05060085 — Mee Siam, standard hawker preparation with tamarind-coconut gravy.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice vermicelli (cooked); composite GI estimated.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 1494mg per serving (360g) — about 65% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by dried shrimp, belacan paste, and light soy seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced sambal and reduced seasoning; the tamarind-based gravy naturally has some acidity that can balance flavour without additional salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a moderate GI (~47); the dish has a moderate glycaemic load per serving (~29); added sugars in the tamarind gravy should be noted.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients may consume Mee Siam occasionally with a reduced noodle portion; the tamarind gravy's added sugar content should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content; tamarind provides antioxidants and the spice base may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefits.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mee Siam is a relatively suitable dish for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the tamarind and spice components may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefits, and the overall fat content is lower than many other hawker dishes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Siam is a Singapore and Malaysian Malay hawker dish of thin rice vermicelli stir-fried in a tangy, mildly spicy, sweet-sour gravy made from dried shrimp, chilli, tamarind, and coconut milk. The dish is garnished with prawns, halved fried tofu, hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, and Chinese chives, finished with a squeeze of lime and a drizzle of sambal. It is considered a uniquely Singaporean-Malay interpretation of Thai-influenced noodle cooking, distinct from Thai recipes, and is commonly found at Malay hawker stalls and food courts.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F05060085 — Mee Siam, standard hawker preparation.
notes: The dish is halal in its standard Malay hawker preparation. Chinese-stall versions may use non-halal ingredients. Sugar content is notably higher than many other noodle dishes due to the sweetened tamarind gravy.
